allergens: gluten (cornstarch may contain traces; soy sauce contains wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Apollo Fish is approximately 150g, equivalent to 6–8 bite-sized fried fish pieces, as commonly served as an appetizer or snack portion in Hyderabadi restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Apollo Fish is a composite dish not listed individually in ICMR-NIN tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for fried fish (Tilapia/Murrel) with adjustments for cornstarch coating, egg batter, deep-frying oil absorption, and spiced yogurt tempering. Core macros cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entries for fried fish and coating ingredients. Values represent a reasonable estimate for this preparation style.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Apollo Fish. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) deep-fried fish with cornstarch coating, analogous to battered/fried fish entries (GI ~38–50) in Atkinson et al. 2008; (2) the high protein content of fish (16.2g/100g) and fat from frying (12.5g/100g) significantly blunting glycemic response of the cornstarch coating (8.5g carbohydrate/100g). Glycemic load per 150g serving = (45 × 8.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.74, rounded to 5.8. Classified as low GI (≤55) and low GL (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450mg/100g; ~675mg per 150g serving) from salt, soy sauce, and seasoning blend
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Apollo Fish consumption due to its high sodium load (~675mg per serving), which represents approximately 29% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg; those on strict low-sodium diets (<1500mg/day) should avoid this dish or request a reduced-sodium preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (estimated 45) and low glycemic load (~5.8 per serving), but deep-fried preparation adds significant fat calories
type2_diabetes.guidance: Apollo Fish has a low estimated GI (~45) and low glycemic load (~5.8 per 150g serving), making it a relatively acceptable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the deep-fried preparation contributes ~18.75g fat per serving and 315 kcal, so portion control and frequency of consumption should be monitored to avoid excess caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and saturated fat; fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids but frying reduces their net benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the fish base provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (estimated ~3.9g PUFA/100g) beneficial for cardiovascular health, the deep-frying process adds saturated fat (~2.8g/100g) and trans fat (~0.1g/100g) and elevates total caloric density; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings and prefer grilled or baked fish preparations as a healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish omega-3 content is anti-inflammatory, but deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory oxidized lipids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids in the fish base may support anti-inflammatory pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management, but the deep-frying process can generate oxidized lipids that may counteract this benefit; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor their individual response and prefer less frequently fried preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apollo Fish is a popular deep-fried seafood appetizer originating from Hyderabad, Telangana, India, widely served in Hyderabadi restaurants and dhabas. The dish features boneless fish fillets — typically Murrel (Channa striata) or Tilapia — cut into bite-sized cubes, marinated in a blend of ginger-garlic paste, egg, cornstarch, and spices, then deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried fish pieces are subsequently tossed in a vibrant tempering of green chilies, curry leaves, garlic, and a yogurt-based sauce, often enhanced with soy sauce and chili paste, giving the dish its characteristic Indo-Chinese fusion profile. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, tender and juicy fish interior, and a bold, spicy-tangy flavor profile with aromatic curry leaf and chili notes. Apollo Fish is nutritionally characterized by high protein content (~16.2g/100g), moderate fat from deep-frying (~12.5g/100g), and relatively low carbohydrates (~8.5g/100g) from the cornstarch coating. It provides meaningful amounts of selenium (~25mcg/100g), phosphorus (~180mg/100g), vitamin B12 (~1.8mcg/100g), and niacin (~3.5mg/100g), reflecting the nutritional profile of fish-based dishes. The sodium content is elevated (~450mg/100g) due to soy sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), supported by the high protein and fat content moderating the glycemic response of the cornstarch coating. Apollo Fish is commonly served as a starter or snack during lunch and dinner, and is a staple party appetizer in Hyderabadi cuisine. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or egg allergies.
common_user_queries: Is Apollo Fish good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Apollo Fish is a well-known Hyderabadi dish but is not individually listed in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried fish data and USDA SR Legacy entries for component ingredients. Core macros are plausible and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact approved-source entry exists for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' for fried fish (Tilapia/Murrel) with adjustments for cornstarch coating, egg batter, deep-frying oil absorption, and spiced yogurt tempering. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fried fish entries). No exact composite dish entry exists in any approved source. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific deep-fried composite preparation is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent dish; detailed moisture data unavailable. 3. Saturated fat (2.8g), monounsaturated_fat_g (4.5g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (3.9g), trans_fat_g (0.1g), and cholesterol_mg (65mg) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried fish and cooking oil data; these are approximate values. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on battered/fried fish entries in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Apollo Fish exists in approved sources. 5. Portions in the input contained three identical duplicate entries (150g); deduplicated to a single default serving of 150g. 6. This dish is not suitable for individuals with fish, egg, soy, or gluten allergies. 7. Apollo Fish is a Hyderabadi regional dish; multilingual coverage includes Hindi (अपोलो फिश) and Telugu (అపోలో ఫిష్) as the primary regional languages. No Chinese, Malay, or Tamil names are applicable as this is not a Singaporean dish.
